data.title: Myanmar's deadly earthquake may bring diplomatic payoff for junta chief
data.description:
Key points:
  • Myanmar locked in civil war following 2021 military coup
  • Death toll from earthquake at nearly 2,900
  • Myanmar says junta chief will attend Bangkok summit
  • Regional powers jostling for influence, analyst says

Myanmar's deadliest natural disaster in years has strengthened the position of ruling general, Min Aung Hlaing, by opening diplomatic channels closed for four years after his junta ousted an elected government to unleash a brutal civil war.

Just before last week's earthquake of magnitude 7.7 that killed almost 2,900 people, the junta chief was readying for a rare foreign visit to a regional summit in Thailand, as aides worked the phones to arrange meetings with other leaders.

Despite the devastation at home, Myanmar state television reported the visit would go ahead, with Min Aung Hlaing set to arrive in Thailand on Thursday ahead of the BIMSTEC grouping's summit in Bangkok on Friday.

The visit, timed just after an outpouring of sympathy following the quake, is a step towards potentially ending his isolation by most world leaders over a war that displaced 3.5 million people and decimated the economy.

"The junta knows that regional powers jostling for influence in Myanmar, like India, China, and Russia would want to use this opportunity to strengthen their own toehold in the country," said Angshuman Choudhury, an analyst based in Singapore.

"By publicly and directly engaging with regional capitals, it can demonstrate its supposed indispensability as Myanmar's primary public authority."

A junta spokesman did not respond to telephone calls from Reuters to seek comment.

In the days since the earthquake, Min Aung Hlaing has spoken with Chinese President Xi Jinping, Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim - conversations that have led to a flurry of international aid.

Just weeks after the junta reaffirmed plans for a general election in December, one of the impoverished nation's strongest earthquakes in a century has opened a new window for its leader to engage with regional powers.

The junta had steadily lost ground in the conflict sparked by the 2021 coup, suffering a string of battlefield defeats and piling unprecedented pressure on Min Aung Hlaing himself.

Key allies such as China have backstopped the junta, with efforts such as pushing major anti-junta groups to stop fighting, but even Beijing had not entirely embraced the embattled general.

He did not get an audience with Xi when he visited China in November for the first time since the coup, for example.

But during a state visit to Moscow last month, Min Aung Hlaing held court with Russian leader Vladimir Putin, one of his earliest backers after the coup.

"I would think he's getting like everything he ever dreamt of and more right now," said a diplomatic source in the commercial capital of Yangon, referring to the Moscow visit, the telephone call with Modi and a meeting of the ASEAN grouping.

"He's back in the circle. He has a seat at the table."

However, the junta is doing what it can to benefit from the crisis and deny assistance to civilians and opposition groups, said a second diplomatic source in the country.

Millions of dollars in aid, relief supplies and hundreds of rescue workers from countries such as China, India, Russia and Southeast Asia, have flooded Myanmar since the quake, though the junta keeps up military operations despite ceasefire calls.

The junta could exploit the crisis to strengthen its position in Myanmar's battlefield, Choudhury added.

"The quake will complicate the resistance's fight and its ability to retain support from the local population."

TIGHTROPE WALK

Some regime hardliners believe the junta can continue with the help of a handful of allies, said Sihasak Phuangketkeow, a former Thai vice minister for foreign affairs who visited Myanmar last month.

"They see the world order as shifting, and that there is a new pole with China, Russia and India," Sihasak told Reuters.

"They think Myanmar may be able to thrive without other engagement."

During the BIMSTEC summit, Min Aung Hlaing would get a further opportunity for diplomatic validation, such as more engagement with India and Thailand.

BIMSTEC, or the Bay of Bengal Initiative for Multi-Sectoral Technical and Economic Cooperation, comprises Thailand, Myanmar, India, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka and Bhutan.

"What I got from my recent trip was that Min Aung Hlaing doesn't want to be just under China only," said Sihasak.

"It is about how we can assert our own position. We should not let this opportunity to engage Myanmar slip by."

In Bangkok, the junta chief could meet Modi, who has rushed aid and personnel to Myanmar since the quake, said three sources with knowledge of discussions.

"Modi, particularly, has already indicated that he is willing to directly engage," said Choudhury, referring to the two leaders' call.

The visit may also give Min Aung Hlaing a chance to meet Thaksin Shinawatra, the former Thai prime minister whom Malaysia's Anwar appointed a personal adviser in his capacity as chairman of ASEAN.

Some analysts say Thailand is walking a tightrope by giving legitimacy to Min Aung Hlaing as he battles the armed resistance which has eroded his grip on Myanmar.

"There's a nasty, brutal, violent civil war," said Thitinan Pongsudhirak, a political scientist at Chulalongkorn University.

"Thailand has to be very careful because it has a long border with Myanmar and lots at stake."

data.title: APRA, RBA Agree to Banks' Use of Overnight Standing Facility
data.description:
The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) and the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) agreed to the use of the RBA's overnight standing facility by banks, the prudential watchdog and the central bank said in a joint statement Wednesday.

APRA and the RBA said they deem this consistent with routine liquidity management activities and will work with banks to ensure they understand the role of the facility and are comfortable using it in their liquidity management practices.

data.title: US 10-Year Treasury Yield Hits 5-1/2 Moth Low
data.description:
The yield on the U.S. 10-year Treasury fell to 4.078%, the lowest since October as investors turned to save heaven assets after President Trump announced higher than expected tariffs on over 180 countries and territories.

Trump administration set a 10% tariff on all imports and higher rates for countries with trade surpluses, including 34% for China, 20% for Europe, and 24% for Japan.

Trump said the U.S. would impose tariffs at about half the rate charged by other countries, factoring in foreign tariffs, trade barriers, and other unfair practices.

data.title: APRA Raises Capital Add-on for ANZ to AU$1 Billion, Accepts Court Enforceable Undertaking; Shares Fall 3%
data.description:
The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) raised the capital add-on applied to ANZ Group Holdings to AU$1 billion from AU$750 billion and accepted a court-enforceable undertaking from the bank, according to a Thursday release.

Under the terms of the undertaking, the bank will appoint an independent reviewer to conduct a group-wide review of the root causes and drivers of ongoing weaknesses in its non-financial risk management practices and risk culture, and to conduct a gap analysis against remediation work.

The bank committed to developing a comprehensive remediation program to address the root causes, which will be independently assessed and reported on a three-monthly basis.

The regulator said it had long-standing concerns over ANZ's non-financial risk management practices and risk culture, including alleged weaknesses in ANZ's operational risk and compliance management and a reactive risk culture.

The results of an independent review, commissioned by the bank and required by APRA, lend credence to the regulator's concerns, it noted.

It added that while ANZ's remediation program is implementing a group-wide nonfinancial risk management framework, system, and operating model, the completion of this program will not effectively address the broader nonfinancial risk weaknesses across the bank.

ANZ's chairman Paul O'Sullivan said the lender recognized that it had more work to do to boost its non-financial risk management and improve its risk culture.

The bank also said that it accepted all recommendations of an independent culture and risk governance review into its global markets business, which were commissioned by its board. It said it would take several actions in response, including appointing senior executives to boots non-financial risk management and risk culture.

The bank's shares fell 3% in recent trading on Thursday.

data.title: Job Vacancies in Australia Fall 4.5% in February
data.description:
Seasonally adjusted job vacancies in Australia fell 4.5% to 328,900 in the three months to February from the prior quarter, the country's bureau of statistics said Thursday.

Private-sector vacancies decreased 5.4% to 291,700, while public-sector vacancies rose 3% to 37,200.

Despite the recent decline, job vacancies were still about 45% higher than they were in February 2020 before the start of the COVID-19 pandemic, the Australian Bureau of Statistics said.

"The December quarter Labour Account continues to show strong demand for people, with vacancies still tracking at around 2.1% of all jobs in Australia. This was unchanged from the previous quarter and was higher than the pre-pandemic rate of 1.6%," said Sean Crick, the bureau's head of labor statistics.

"Compared with a year ago, job vacancies fell in 11 industries, with the largest drops in Construction (-33.8%) and Education and training (-26%). The strongest percentage growth in job vacancies over the year was in Rental, hiring and real estate services (+13.0%)," Crick added.

Job vacancies in most industries remained higher than they were before the COVID-19 pandemic. Accommodation and food services, and arts and recreation services both remained particularly high compared with their pre-pandemic levels.

Compared with the year-earlier period, job vacancies were down 9.3%.

data.title: U.S. Tariffs Forecast to Lower China Growth by 2.4 Pct Pts, Citigroup SaysMarket Talk
data.description: The U.S. tariff rate on Chinese goods, set to rise to around 65% cumulatively with the latest reciprocal tariffs, is expected to reduce China's economic growth by 2.4 percentage points and lower exports growth by 15.4 percentage points, according to Citigroup estimates. The investment bank says the latest tariff hike "is almost a prohibitive level" for U.S.-China trade and the estimation of impact on the Chinese economy has been made before considering any offsetting measures. The latest move is unlikely to bode well for negotiations between the two nations and Beijing's response will be more substantial than for the fentanyl tariffs, Citigroup says in a note. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

data.title: Trump Officials Gave Conflicting Tariff Rates for China. White House Calls the Issue a 'Pointless Aside.'
data.description:
By Matt Peterson and Reshma Kapadia

What does President Donald Trump's new tariff regime mean for the all-important trading relationship with China — the country that has the widest trade deficit with the U.S.?

In the immediate aftermath of Trump's tariff announcement, confusion reigned even among some White House officials about what rate the approximately $440 billion in Chinese imports would face. Policy experts were perplexed, too.

"They haven't provided their homework," said Andy Rothman, a longtime China watcher and head of China-focused Sinology.

Trump started the trade war with China in his first term as president and has since imposed an array of duties on the world's second-largest economy. In his first term, he imposed tariffs of up to 25% on $350 billion of goods. The Biden administration left those in place and raised some further.

In February, Trump imposed 10% of additional tariffs on all Chinese imports over its involvement in the fentanyl trade. He then raised those fentanyl-based tariffs by another 10%.

Coming into Wednesday's announcement, that meant the tariff rate on some goods from China would be 45%. How does the raft of new tariffs change that?

A briefing for reporters held in advance of Trump's announcement didn't detail the new rate for China under the so-called reciprocal tariffs, though officials said that tariffs would stack on top of previous ones. The briefing, held on the condition that reporters not use the names of the officials involved, left it to the president to spell that all out. A senior administration official in that meeting told reporters that the rates "are customized to each country" based on "very, very well established methodologies."

Trump mentioned China in his remarks but didn't specify a number. However, a list of individual tariffs handed out at the event and posted on the government's social media accounts did. The administration calculated that China was effectively charging the U.S. 67%, citing tariffs as well as non-tariff barriers that range from currency manipulation and export subsidies to unfair rules and creating "pollution havens." It then cut that figure in half — Trump described it as a "discount" — and rounded up to 34%.

But the president nor the executive order provided clarity on whether this number was in addition to the existing 45% in levies on imports from China.

Responding to a question from a Bloomberg TV reporter about whether the rate for China would be more than 50%, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said yes. But the reporter didn't ask about the 25% tariffs in place from his first term. Including Wednesday's announcement, Trump has added 54% in new tariffs in his second term.

Barron's asked the White House whether the 34% reciprocal tariff gets added to the existing 45%. Yes, a White House official who asked not to be quoted by name said in an email. The new rate was 34% plus 45%, or 79%, for those goods already subject to the full tariffs.

Hold on, the official emailed back 10 minutes later. The rate for China isn't 79% but 54%, because the "Venezuela tariffs" aren't in effect yet, the official wrote. That was apparently a reference to Trump's threat to impose 25% tariffs on countries, including China, that import Venezuelan oil.

What about the 25% tariffs from the first term? "You're the first one to ask me that," the White House press official said when called for clarification. The official hung up, did some checking, and phoned back.

The final answer: The U.S. will be charging some Chinese goods as much as 79% once the new tariff package goes into effect.

"With everything else that unfolded today this seems like a pointless aside to spend any energy on," the official said. "But the rules and systems that govern international trade were incredibly complex to begin with — never mind action to initiate a historic realignment of how the U.S. fits into global trade."

Whether these reciprocal tariffs would stack up or not was one of the key questions flagged by investors and analysts ahead of the announcement. After the event, Henrietta Treyz, director of economic policy research at Veda Partners, said the uncertainty about China's rate stood out. Sinology's Rothman was also confused.

The lack of detail about what went into the reciprocal rate could complicate China's ability to respond, Rothman said.

Write to Matt Peterson at matt.peterson@dowjones.com and Reshma Kapadia at reshma.kapadia@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2133 ET - The U.S. tariff rate on Chinese goods, set to rise to around 65% cumulatively with the latest reciprocal tariffs, is expected to reduce China's economic growth by 2.4 percentage points and lower exports growth by 15.4 percentage points, according to Citigroup estimates. The investment bank says the latest tariff hike "is almost a prohibitive level" for U.S.-China trade and the estimation of impact on the Chinese economy has been made before considering any offsetting measures. The latest move is unlikely to bode well for negotiations between the two nations and Beijing's response will be more substantial than for the fentanyl tariffs, Citigroup says in a note. (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

2125 ET - Australia's agriculture, manufacturing and wine exports are at greatest risk of a direct impact from President Trump's sweeping new tariffs, says Emanuel Datt, founder and chief investment officer at Datt Capital. However, a much greater impact on Australia is likely to come from how the tariffs impact countries such as China, Japan and South Korea, which account for a substantial portion of Australia's exports, Datt says. "Although the 10% baseline tariff was less than anticipated, the imposition of far steeper tariffs on countries such as China (54%), Japan (24%) and Vietnam (46%) has raised fears of prolonged economic disruption and intensified uncertainty about the future of global trade," he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2124 ET - Trump's additional tariffs on its global partners could potentially raise U.S. average tariff rates to levels not seen since the early 20th century, Tai Hui, APAC Chief Market Strategist, J.P. Morgan Asset Management writes in an email. If these tariffs persist, it would materially impact inflation as U.S. manufacturing struggles to ramp up capacity and supply chains pass on costs to consumers, Tai says. The scale of these tariffs raises concerns about U.S. growth risks since domestic consumers may cut back on spending given more expensive imports, he adds. Businesses may delay capital expenditures given uncertainty on the tariffs' full impact and potential retaliation from trade partners, he adds. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2123 ET - Australian debt markets have experienced a dovish pivot since the White House announced its new tariff agenda. There is now an 85% chance of a 25-basis-point RBA interest-rate cut priced in for May, says Tony Sycamore, market analyst at IG. Follow-up cuts are priced for August and November with a cumulative 75 bps of cuts priced by November. The tariff announcements went far beyond what was expected, and have increased the chances of a trade war and recession in the U.S., he adds. With goods from countries like China and Vietnam now effectively shut out of the U.S., expect to see a flood of cheaper goods into other Asian markets, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2118 ET - Economists at Morgan Stanley say the drag on growth from Trump's reciprocal tariffs on Asia could be greater than in 2018-19, with Asia the most exposed to tariffs given that most countries in the region run a trade surplus with the U.S. The focus will be on whether those countries will be able to negotiate deals with the U.S. to lower tariffs, they say in a note to clients. "Elevated levels of policy uncertainty will weigh on corporate confidence and consequently capex and trade", MS' economists say; "We think the more trade-oriented an economy is, the bigger the growth drag will be." (singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

2113 ET - Companies will now have a lot at stake in parsing the country of origin for goods to be imported into the U.S., says Dorsey & Whitney partner David Townsend. "President Trump's reciprocal tariffs create significant differential tariff rates by the country of origin," says Townsend. "For goods that undergo processing or assembly in multiple countries, this creates large differences in tariff liability based on correctly applying the rules of origin." Whether the sweeping new tariffs remain in place for the long term is impossible to predict, he says. "The executive order establishing these reciprocal tariffs makes President Trump the final arbiter of whether the tariffs are revoked, modified, or increased," says Townsend. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2105 ET - The Reserve Bank of Australia's latest report card on the country's banking sector has warned that ongoing uncertainty about U.S. trade policies "could have a chilling effect on business investment and household spending decisions, and pose substantial headwinds to the outlook for global economic activity and inflation." There is also considerable uncertainty about the effects of possible fiscal, regulatory and other government policy changes on global growth and inflation, the central bank says. The comments come after the RBA left the official interest rate on hold this week, despite some economists warning that U.S. tariffs could stall the global economy and fuel inflation. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2101 ET - The key for markets now is how other countries respond to President Trump's sweeping tariffs, says Niv Dagan, executive director at Peak Asset Management. "Markets don't like uncertainty," says Dagan, who expects a continued flight from risk into gold and other defensive assets. The Melbourne-based asset manager reckons the impact of U.S. tariffs on Australian goods is unlikely to be significant. "Most Australian exports do not get sent to the U.S.," but rather to China, Japan and South Korea, he says. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2058 ET - Trump's high tariffs on Asean countries including Vietnam and Cambodia risk denting China's exports to those countries, says Zhiwei Zhang, Pinpoint Asset Management. The supply chains in East Asia are vertically integrated, with China's exports of intermediate goods key for these countries' exporters, notes Zhang. "A key question going forward is how the U.S. and these countries will negotiate tariffs," Zhang says, adding that Trump's new tariffs pose significant risk to global trade and supply chains in East Asia.(singaporeeditors@dowjones.com)

2035 ET - Fiscal stimulus from all levels of Canada's governments could help ease the sting from President Trump's new tariff regime, says TD Bank chief economist Beata Caranci. Still, total stimulus of 2% of GDP, or about C$480 billion, "won't prevent near-term economic stagnation" as companies absorb the President's policy shock, she says. "Canada will need to brace for a long period of economic restructuring," Caranci says. "Canadians cannot 'unsee' the past three months" of heightened uncertainty posed by Trump's repeated threats of 25% tariffs. Canada avoided new duties from Trump's "Liberation Day" reveal. Inflation in Canada is expected to top 3% by the summer, Caranci predicts, but she believes there is room for at least two additional quarter-point cuts from the Bank of Canada this year. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

2033 ET - AUD​/USD ​has ranged through a high ​of 0.6341​ to a low of low of 0.6226​ since U.S. President Trump announced a sweeping new tariff regime on his country's trading partners. The pair now trades at 0.6259. It amounts to only a modest net decline for the ​AUD, with Australia escaping the tariff hike relatively lightly, and with lots of bad news already priced into the currency​, says Sean Callow, currency analyst at InTouch Capital Markets. Traders will be bracing for China's response​ through the USD/CNY fix, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2027 ET - JGB yields slide on U.S. President Trump's tariff announcement overnight. "Risk-off should be a common theme, and should lower market rates," say two members of ING's Economic and Financial Analysis Division in a research report. To the extent that U.S. Treasury yields drop as a result of the tariffs, there's downward pressure on global rates and a tightening versus lower yielders, the members add. The 10-year JGB yield is down 10bps at 1.370% after earlier touching 1.340%, the lowest intraday level since February 26, according to data provider Quick.(ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Tariffs Could Reduce China's U.S. Trade Surplus to Zero, ANZ SaysMarket Talk
data.description: Increased U.S. tariffs on China overnight could lead to zero trade surplus from China, ANZ Research analyst Zhaopeng Xing says. The impact of the new tariffs on China's current trade surplus will be equivalent to about 1.5 percentage points of its GDP, he adds. Considering that this year's general deficit was risen, the tariffs may not affect China's 5% GDP growth goal. However, the levies mark a crucial change for global trade and economic structure that may affect trade flows for many years, ANZ says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Brazilian Real Gains as U.S. Spares Country from Harsh Tariffs
data.description:
The Brazilian real hovered around 5.66 per USD, strengthening for the third consecutive session after Brazil avoided being labeled a "bad actor" on trade by the White House.

As a result, the country was spared harsher penalties and will face only a 10% baseline tariff on exports to the U.S. Despite Brazil’s historically high tariffs on imports, the list announced by President Trump on Wednesday showed that Brazil imposes just a 10% tariff on U.S. goods—the lowest rate among the cited countries—while receiving the same 10% reciprocal tariff in return.

Brazil now hopes to position itself as a relative winner in the escalating trade war, leveraging global tensions to increase commodity exports to China and other nations that are scaling back trade with the U.S

data.title: Market Chatter: BHP Group Explored Spin-Off of Australian Iron Ore, Coal Units
data.description:
BHP Group explored the separation of its Australian iron ore and coal units, including a potential listing, as part of the mining giant's medium-term growth strategy of focusing on potash and copper, Reuters reported Wednesday, citing three people familiar with the matter.

Talks of a spin-off were underway amid the company's efforts to lower its carbon emissions and its takeover bid for Anglo American in 2023 and 2024, Reuters said.

BHP's South Australian copper assets were excluded from such discussions, per the report.

In the end, the company decided to abort the plan as the revenue generated from the two units was necessary to fund the Escondida copper complex in Chile and the Jansen potash development in Canada, according to Reuters.

BHP did not immediately respond to a request for comment by MT Newswires.

data.title: ChiNext Price Index Opens 1.3% Lower at 2078.15
Australia's Galan Lithium declines $150 million buyout bid for Argentine assets
data.description:
Australia's Galan Lithium said on Thursday that it rejected an offer from Zhejiang Huayou Cobalt Co and Renault Group to acquire the lithium explorer's stake in its Argentine assets for $150 million in cash.

Galan Lithium described the offer as "opportunistic" and "undervalued", adding that it was for the purchase of the company's entire interest in the Hombre Muerto West (HMW) and Candelas projects in Argentina.

Shares of Galan were up 38.1% at A$0.145, as of 0111 GMT, while the broader benchmark index was down 1.5%. The stock climbed to its highest level since early January.

Last August, EnergyX, a lithium technology startup company, made a $150 million offer to purchase Galan's Argentine assets.

The Hombre Muerto West project, Galan's flagship project, is located near lithium projects and owned by Arcadium Lithium (ALTM.N) and Posco Holdings . Posco is an investor in EnergyX.

data.title: Japanese Yen Rallies Amid Trade War Concerns
data.description:
The Japanese yen appreciated past 148 per dollar on Thursday, hitting a three-week high as investors sought safe-haven assets after US President Donald Trump announced sweeping reciprocal tariffs, escalating fears of a disruptive global trade war.

Trump imposed an additional 34% tariff on China, raising total levies on the country to 54%.

Other major economies facing hefty tariffs include the EU (20%), Japan (24%), and India (26%), alongside a 10% baseline tariff on imports from all countries.

Earlier this week, Bank of Japan Governor Kazuo Ueda warned that the new US tariffs could significantly impact global trade and economic growth.

While the BOJ is expected to raise interest rates further later this year, uncertainty over global trade and domestic economic conditions continues to weigh on the outlook.

data.title: Breville Flags Higher Costs, Possible Change to Manufacturing Plan After U.S. Tariff Announcement
data.description:
By Mike Cherney

SYDNEY--Australia-based appliance maker Breville Group, which makes most of its products in China, said costs are likely to increase and that it could adjust a manufacturing plan after the U.S. announced across-the-board reciprocal tariffs.

The company said it is likely that its "input costs" will increase for the 2026 fiscal year, and that it will make tactical adjustments to lessen the short-term impacts from the tariffs.

It said it is already well progressed on a project to diversify its manufacturing base, with the initial target locations being Mexico, Indonesia and Cambodia. It said the project, however, may adjust as facts on the ground evolve.

President Trump earlier announced a 10% reciprocal tariff on many countries, though some will be subject to significantly higher rates. China will face a 34% reciprocal tariff, for example, and Cambodia will face a 49% reciprocal tariff.

Breville said it currently manufactures about 90% of its products by value in China, with the rest coming from the European Union, Mexico and Taiwan. The company said it also sells some 45% of its products into the U.S.

The company added that it doesn't expect a material impact from the U.S. tariffs on its fiscal 2025 result, and reiterated its fiscal 2025 guidance for Ebit growth of 5%-10%.

Breville shares were down some 7% in recent trade to about 29.40 Australian dollars, equivalent to $18.44, the fifth worst performing stock on Australia's main index.

Write to Mike Cherney at mike.cherney@wsj.com

data.title: Shareholders that lost money on Sana Biotechnology, Inc.(SANA) should contact Levi & Korsinsky about pending Class Action - SANA
data.description:
NEW YORK, NY / ACCESS Newswire / April 2, 2025 / If you suffered a loss on your Sana Biotechnology, Inc. investment and want to learn about a potential recovery under the federal securities laws, follow the link below for more information:

https://zlk.com/pslra-1/sana-biotechnology-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140614&wire=1

or contact Joseph E. Levi, Esq. via email at jlevi@levikorsinsky.com or call (212) 363-7500 to speak to our team of experienced shareholder advocates.

THE LAWSUIT: A class action securities lawsuit was filed against Sana Biotechnology, Inc. that seeks to recover losses of shareholders who were adversely affected by alleged securities fraud between March 17, 2023 and November 4, 2024.

CASE DETAILS: The filed complaint alleges that defendants made false statements and/or concealed that: (i) Sana was at significant risk of having insufficient funds to maintain its current operations and advance one or more of its product candidates; (ii) SC291 in oncology, SC379, and SG299 were less promising than defendants had led investors to believe; (iii) in order to preserve cash and advance its more promising product candidates, Sana was likely to decrease funding for and/or discontinue SC291 in oncology, SC379, and SG299, as well as significantly reduce its headcount; (iv) accordingly, defendants overstated Sana's financial capacity to maintain its current operations and advance its existing product candidates; and (v) as a result, defendants' public statements were materially false and/or misleading at all relevant times.

WHAT'S NEXT? If you suffered a loss in Sana Biotechnology, Inc. stock during the relevant time frame - even if you still hold your shares - go to https://zlk.com/pslra-1/sana-biotechnology-inc-lawsuit-submission-form?prid=140614&wire=1 to learn about your rights to seek a recovery. There is no cost or obligation to participate.
